word_list.pronunciation: 蓯伞藞蓻nst 藞蓴藧l 冒瑟 藞蓴藧l 冒蓹 藞蓱藧dz

senses.definition: used to describe a situation where something or someone succeeds despite facing very difficult or unlikely circumstances

word_list.pronunciation: 忙t 藢蓻ni 晒藞e瑟t

senses.definition: used to indicate that the speaker is moving on to another point or topic

examples.x: We may not have all the details yet, but at any rate, we need to start planning for the upcoming event.
At any rate, let's focus on finding a solution to the problem at hand.
I'm not sure if she'll be able to make it to the meeting, but at any rate, we can update her later.
It's unclear what caused the delay, but at any rate, we need to meet the deadline.
At any rate, let's move forward with the project and see how it develops.

word_list.pronunciation: 瑟n 蓴藧晒 ba瑟 k藞蓱藧nt晒忙st

senses.definition: used to highlight the differences between two or more things or people

examples.x: The old house had a charming, rustic feel, whereas the new one, in contrast, is sleek and modern.
In the summer, the city is bustling with tourists and outdoor events; by contrast, the winter months are quiet and subdued.
The professor's lectures are informative and engaging; by contrast, the textbook is dry and difficult to understand.
The first half of the movie was action-packed and fast-paced; in contrast, the second half was slow and introspective.
The two siblings have very different personalities鈥擳om is outgoing and sociable, while his sister Emily is shy and reserved, by contrast.

word_list.pronunciation: 蓱藧n冒蓹 k藞蓱藧nt晒蓻晒i

senses.definition: used to indicate that the opposite or a different viewpoint is true in response to a previous statement

examples.x: John believed the project would fail, but, on the contrary, it was a great success.

word_list.pronunciation: 蓱藧n冒蓹 fl藞瑟p s藞a瑟d

senses.definition: used to introduce a contrasting aspect of a situation

word_list.pronunciation: 蓱藧n冒蓹 w藞蕦n h藞忙nd

senses.definition: used to introduce one aspect of a situation, often followed by a contrasting statement

examples.x: On the one hand, the new policy promotes efficiency, but on the other hand, it may reduce employee morale.
Working in a fast-paced environment offers opportunities for growth and advancement, but on the one hand, it can lead to stress and burnout.
Investing in stocks has the potential for high returns, but on the one hand, it also carries the risk of financial loss.
Using social media allows for instant connection and networking, but on the one hand, it can also lead to addiction and negative impacts on mental health.
On the one hand, traveling abroad exposes you to new cultures and experiences, but on the other hand, it can be expensive and logistically challenging.

word_list.pronunciation: 蓱藧n冒瑟 藞蕦冒蓺 h藞忙nd 蓴藧晒

senses.definition: used to introduce a contrasting aspect of a situation, especially when comparing it to a previous point

examples.x: The job pays well; on the other hand, the hours are extremely long.

word_list.pronunciation: 瑟n 晒藢蓻t晒o蕣sp藞蓻kt

senses.definition: used to reconsider something after gaining more information or experience

examples.x: In retrospect, I realize that taking that job was a great career move.
In retrospect, I see now that quitting my job without a backup plan wasn't the wisest choice.
In retrospect, studying abroad was one of the best experiences of my life; it broadened my perspective in so many ways.
In retrospect, I wish I had spent more time with my family when I had the chance.
In retrospect, buying that house was a great decision; its value has appreciated significantly over the years.
